universal_rubrics.name: Company Scale Similarity
universal_rubrics.description: Measures whether the candidate has experience working in organizations with scale comparable to the hiring company.

levels.poor: Candidate worked only in very small or unrelated scale environments compared to the target company.
levels.borderline: Candidate has limited exposure to larger systems or teams but not direct ownership at scale.
levels.good: Candidate worked in companies with similar scale in terms of engineering teams, customers, or product complexity.
levels.strong: Candidate demonstrated success operating at equal or larger scale organizations (large user base, enterprise systems, high traffic products).

universal_rubrics.name: Industry / Domain Alignment
universal_rubrics.description: Measures similarity between candidate industry experience and the job's product or technology domain.

levels.poor: No relevant industry or product domain experience.
levels.borderline: Adjacent domain experience but limited overlap with target industry.
levels.good: Relevant industry exposure with transferable product or technical knowledge.
levels.strong: Direct experience solving problems within the same industry or product category.

universal_rubrics.name: Execution Environment Fit
universal_rubrics.description: Measures whether the candidate has experience working in environments with similar speed, complexity, and iteration cycles.

levels.poor: Experience mainly in slow-moving environments with limited product or engineering iteration.
levels.borderline: Some exposure to agile or iterative environments but limited evidence of fast delivery.
levels.good: Regular experience delivering work in agile teams with frequent releases or product iterations.
levels.strong: Proven ability to operate in high-velocity environments with rapid experimentation and product delivery.

universal_rubrics.name: Culture Fit Signals
universal_rubrics.description: Signals indicating collaboration, ownership, communication, and leadership alignment with company culture.

levels.poor: Resume shows isolated responsibilities with minimal collaboration.
levels.borderline: Basic collaboration with limited cross-functional interaction.
levels.good: Demonstrates strong collaboration across teams such as engineering, product, and business.
levels.strong: Evidence of leadership, cross-functional influence, mentorship, or driving organizational alignment.

universal_rubrics.name: Career Trajectory & Seniority Alignment
universal_rubrics.description: Evaluates whether the candidate’s experience and growth trajectory matches the seniority expected in the JD.

levels.poor: Candidate experience significantly below expected role level.
levels.borderline: Some relevant experience but limited leadership or ownership signals.
levels.good: Experience aligns well with the role level and responsibilities.
levels.strong: Candidate demonstrates progression, leadership growth, and responsibilities exceeding role expectations.

resume.name: Product Strategy Ownership
resume.description: Measures the candidate’s ownership of product direction and strategic planning.

levels.poor: No ownership of product roadmap or strategic planning; mostly execution tasks.
levels.borderline: Some involvement in roadmap discussions but limited ownership.
levels.good: Owns roadmap decisions and prioritization aligned with business goals.
levels.strong: Defines multi-quarter product strategy with measurable business outcomes.

resume.name: Product Delivery & Execution
resume.description: Measures ability to deliver products or features successfully.

levels.poor: No evidence of shipping products or features.
levels.borderline: Participation in launches without clear ownership.
levels.good: Delivered multiple features or products with measurable adoption.
levels.strong: Led complex product launches with significant business impact.

interview.name: Product Sense & User Empathy
interview.description: Ability to think through product problems from a user perspective during the interview.

levels.poor: Struggles to identify user pain points or logical solutions.
levels.borderline: Identifies basic needs but lacks structured thinking.
levels.good: Structured approach to user needs and feature prioritization.
levels.strong: Exceptional clarity in user discovery and innovative solutioning.

interview.name: Strategic Trade-off Reasoning
interview.description: Ability to justify complex product decisions under constraints.

levels.borderline: Mentions constraints but lacks clear prioritization framework.
levels.good: Clear reasoning for trade-offs between speed, cost, and quality.
levels.strong: Nuanced understanding of long-term vs short-term business impact.

resume.name: Technical Stack Alignment
resume.description: Measures alignment between JD technology requirements and candidate experience.

levels.good: Hands-on experience with most technologies required by the JD.
levels.strong: Deep expertise with the tech stack including optimization or architecture work.

resume.name: System Scale Experience
resume.description: Measures experience with large-scale or production systems.

levels.poor: Only small projects or academic systems mentioned.
levels.good: Built or maintained production systems with measurable scale.
levels.strong: Designed distributed systems handling high traffic or large datasets.

interview.name: System Architecture Thinking
interview.description: Ability to design scalable systems and handle edge cases during technical rounds.

levels.poor: Struggles with basic component interaction or bottleneck identification.
levels.borderline: Basic design works but fails under high scale or specific failures.
levels.good: Solid design with clear components, APIs, and scaling strategy.
levels.strong: Sophisticated architecture with deep understanding of CAP theorem and tradeoffs.

interview.name: Analytical Logic & Bug Resilience
interview.description: Precision in implementation and ability to debug complex logic.

levels.poor: Slow implementation, basic syntax errors, or logical flaws.
levels.borderline: Solved problem but needed significant prompting for edge cases.
levels.good: Clean, efficient implementation with good handle on edge cases.
levels.strong: Exceptional speed, elegance, and proactive testing of the solution.

resume.name: Program Execution & Scale
resume.description: Observable track record of delivering end-to-end complex programs.

levels.poor: No evidence of owning end-to-end project lifecycles.
levels.borderline: Managed small projects but limited evidence of cross-functional scale.
levels.good: Delivered multi-month programs across multiple departments.
levels.strong: Consistently delivers high-stakes, enterprise-scale programs with clear metrics.

resume.name: Technical Breadth & Domain Fit
resume.description: Evidence of working in technical environments similar to the JD.

levels.borderline: Generalist background with limited depth in the required tech domain.
levels.good: Solid understanding of the relevant tech stack and engineering processes.
levels.strong: Deep domain expertise that allows for meaningful technical pushback.

interview.name: Proactive Risk Sensing
interview.description: Ability to see around corners and preemptively manage bottlenecks during case studies.

levels.poor: Reacts to issues only after they are mentioned.
levels.borderline: Identifies obvious risks but misses subtle sequence bottlenecks.
levels.good: Strong proactive planning and fallback strategy development.
levels.strong: Exceptional instinct for critical-path risk and mitigation sequencing.

interview.name: Influence & Stakeholder Negotiation
interview.description: Ability to drive consensus among conflicting engineering and product goals.

levels.poor: Passive or overly aggressive; fails to build consensus.
levels.borderline: Can follow up but struggles to persuade senior stakeholders.
levels.good: Effective at building alignment and resolving low-stakes conflicts.
levels.strong: Masterful negotiator; drives high-stakes alignment while maintaining trust.
